Skip to main content

RealDoor

Get your paperwork ready. A person decides the rest.

We get your file to the person who decides, complete the first time you hand it over. What we cannot tell you is the outcome — and no software can, because that decision needs checks that are not in these documents. Nothing on this screen means approved, denied, or ineligible; a qualified housing professional decides that.

Check the values we read from your documents

RealDoor reads a document you give it. It shows you where on the page every value came from; choose a field name to light up its box. If a value is wrong, fix it on its row — the numbers that depend on it are worked out again, in place.

Or open a prepared example file

If you would rather look around first, this copy carries six made-up household files. They belong to nobody. Everything both pages do — the evidence boxes, the corrections, the checklist, the packet — works the same on them as on a document of your own.

Data source: loading…

See what your file adds up to, then take your packet

Three things, in order. First, the yearly figure we worked out, and the frozen limit it is compared against. Then the checklist: what is present, what is missing, what is out of date. Last, the packet you hand to the housing office. A comparison is not a determination.

The calculation Missing or expired Your packet

How your yearly figure was worked out

What is missing or out of date

Check what we found, then take your packet

Not part of the renter's walkthrough

How this works, and how we tested it

This page is for anyone checking the service rather than using it. Every control below can be triggered here and its actual response shown — a disclaimer is not a control. Underneath are our own measurements, printed as they came out.

Before you start

This walkthrough takes about ten minutes. The prepared example files are already loaded, so you can go straight through it without uploading anything. Page 1 also lets you read a synthetic document of your own, held in memory for this session only. Nothing is sent anywhere. You can stop at any point and your work stays on this device.

What happens, in order

The renter's walkthrough is two pages, in the order they are presented. It opens on page 1, so this list is a description of it rather than a gate in front of it. It used to be six ordered step-screens; the owner's call was that six screens were too big an obstacle, so the same work now lives on two pages and nothing was dropped.

    Where the challenge's six acceptance steps live

    The challenge brief specifies a six-step acceptance demo. Our walkthrough is two pages written for the renter, not for that list, so the two do not line up one to one. This is the mapping, and every acceptance step remains individually demonstrable.

    • 1. Upload documents, show extraction evidence — page 1. The upload panel is the front door, and one press opens a prepared example instead; either way every value carries the box on the page it came from. An uploaded document is read on its own; the session's uploads together form a file of their own that both pages can walk.
    • 2. Edit one field, show downstream values update — page 1. “This is wrong — fix it” opens an editor on the row itself; when the correction commits, the before/after summary — the recomputed figure, the threshold move, or the recorded-but-not-used explanation — appears in place under that document's table.
    • 3. Ask a rule question, show an authoritative citation — the ask box pinned at the foot of every page. Its answers carry the rule id, authority, effective date and source; the recorded questions, including the eligibility refusal, are in the “Recorded questions” list under the same box.
    • 4. Deterministic calculation with effective dates — page 2, “How your yearly figure was worked out”.
    • 5. Identify missing or expired items, export a packet — page 2, “What is missing or out of date” and then “Check what we found, then take your packet”, top to bottom on the same page.
    • 6. Run the refusal, prompt-injection and session-deletion tests — this page, immediately below. It is not part of the walkthrough because it is not a task the renter performs. Both the adversarial suite and the static no-decision guard exercise these paths on every run.

    Why the screens are worded the way they are

    The open-questions rail is always visible and never folded away. A system that knows something and does not say it is the failure this product exists to prevent, so the things we are unsure about get a permanent rail rather than a disclosure the renter has to think to open.

    An empty list means nothing was withheld, not that nothing was checked. When a household's abstentions list comes back empty, that is a measured result — every value needed was read from a document and every required item is accounted for — and not the absence of a measurement.

    We say what we cannot do, and why we cannot do it. The service reports readiness and never eligibility. That is not a decision we are holding back: an eligibility determination needs third-party income verification, household-composition proof and status checks that do not exist in a document set, so no software can produce one from this input. What we can do is make the handoff to the person who does decide complete the first time. Getting a file there sooner is not the same as getting a better answer out of it, and we do not claim the second.

    Rule questions run on deterministic handlers, and no document text reaches the calculation. An answer to a rule question is computed from frozen rules and returned with its citation; no document, no extracted field and no household record is part of that computation. This line used to sit above the ask box on every screen, where it explained the machinery to a renter who only wanted to ask a question.

    What folds and what may not. On page 2 each section leads with its answer and folds its full working — inputs, formulas, rule citations, the satisfied checklist items — behind a disclosure. Abstentions, review reasons and any figure we could not compute never fold: what is settled collapses, what is open stays loud with its next step. That is the same rule the packet follows.

    The data behind this demo

    The households in the picker are synthetic. The renter's screens call this an example household with sample documents, which is what it is; the technical version is that this build ships ui/dist/fixtures.js, captured from a real run of the same pipeline that serves the live API, so the offline page shows genuine extraction output with no server and no network behind it.

    The data source in use is named under the household picker on every screen. If a server is running and you want the offline fixtures instead, add ?fixtures to the URL.

    The controls, demonstrated live

    Our own numbers

    Our measured results, including the parts we did not run. Published as measured, whether or not they flatter us.

    Ask about a housing rule

    You can ask from either page, in your own words. The answer opens below, with the rule id, the authority behind it and the date it took effect. The recorded questions this copy can always answer are in the list underneath.

    Recorded questions

    What this system is unsure about

    Anything we could not read, or could not be sure of, is listed here — on every screen, whichever page you are on.

    About this build

    This page sends nothing to outside servers on its own. When the question classifier is switched on, your typed question — and only that — goes to a model provider so it can pick a topic; no document, no extracted field and no household record ever does.

    Technical details

    No external fonts, scripts, images, or analytics. Every request this page makes goes to the address in the bar above it and nowhere else; opened as a file, it makes none at all. That is a promise about this page, not about the whole system: when the question classifier is switched on, the server sends your question sentence to a model provider so it can pick a topic label. What we can promise structurally is narrower and harder: no document, no extracted field and no household record is ever part of that request. Before your sentence is sent we replace the identifier shapes we can recognise, such as an email address, a phone number or a street address. We cannot recognise all of them, so a name you type is sent as you typed it.
